summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case that AMD's Athlon 64 processor is the optimal choice for .NET developers due to its significantly faster compilation times compared to Intel's Pentium 4. He presents benchmark data showing a 33% speed advantage at equivalent clock speeds, arguing that even the cheapest Athlon 64 outperforms the fastest Pentium 4 for compilation. Beyond development work, he notes additional benefits including gaming performance, upcoming 64-bit Windows support, and hardware security features. The post concludes with practical purchasing advice and a critique of Dell's Intel exclusivity.
key_points: Athlon 64 compiles .NET code 33% faster than equivalently-rated Pentium 4 processors
The cheapest Athlon 64 outperforms the most expensive Pentium 4 for compilation tasks
Compile time represents accumulated productivity loss since it's too short to context-switch but long enough to notice
AMD offers additional advantages: gaming performance, 64-bit support, and no-execute bit security features
Dell's exclusive Intel partnership prevents them from offering what Atwood considers superior technology
money_quotes: For compiling .NET code, the Athlon 64 is 33% faster than a Pentium 4 of the same speed.
Time spent compiling is time wasted staring at the build output window.

Compiling doesn't typically take long enough to force me to ALT+TAB away and start doing something else, so there's no real incentive to multitask... and those wasted seconds add up throughout the day.
You could buy the cheapest Athlon 64 available and still get better .NET compiler performance than the fastest Pentium 4 available.
That's cash in your pocket twice: once for the purchase of the less expensive CPU, and again as you spend less time writing the same code.
Dell has some kind of satanic pact with Intel, and even though the Athlon 64 is a superior technology – in pretty much everything except for media encoding – they will never offer it.
key_insight: Developer hardware choices should be driven by compilation performance benchmarks, as the accumulated seconds of waiting for builds represent a hidden but significant productivity cost.
